questions.question: What are automatic thoughts, and why do they matter for emotional wellbeing?

options.text: They're deliberate, conscious thoughts you choose — they matter because you can control them
They're rapid, involuntary interpretations that drive emotions — often without your awareness
They're always negative and should be suppressed
They're background noise that doesn't significantly affect your mood

questions.explanation: Correct. Automatic thoughts are the mental commentary that runs constantly in response to situations. They feel like facts, not interpretations, which makes them highly believable — and highly influential on how you feel.

questions.question: What is 'mind-reading,' and why is it considered a cognitive distortion?

options.text: Accurately perceiving others' emotions through body language and tone
Assuming you know what others are thinking, usually that they're judging you negatively, without evidence
A useful skill for avoiding conflict by anticipating others' needs
Believing that others can read your thoughts

questions.explanation: Yes. Mind-reading is when you treat your assumptions about others' thoughts as facts ('they think I'm incompetent'), often generating anxiety or shame based on interpretations you haven't verified.

questions.question: What is the purpose of thought monitoring in CBT?

options.text: To stop automatic thoughts from occurring
To catch automatic thoughts as they occur, write them down, and identify distortions
To analyze your childhood experiences and understand where negative thoughts came from
To replace all negative thoughts with positive affirmations

questions.explanation: Exactly. Thought monitoring builds awareness. You can't challenge a thought you haven't noticed. By catching and recording automatic thoughts, you make them visible and testable.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of 'catastrophizing'?

options.text: 'I made a mistake in that email — my boss will think I'm careless'
'I didn't get the job — I'll never find work and I'll end up homeless'
'I failed this test — I'm bad at this subject'
'I feel anxious, so this situation must be dangerous'

questions.explanation: Yes, this is catastrophizing. You're predicting the worst possible outcome (homelessness) from a single event (not getting one job) and treating it as inevitable.

questions.question: Think of a recent situation where you felt a strong negative emotion (anxiety, sadness, frustration). What was your automatic thought in that moment? Can you identify which cognitive distortion it reflected?
questions.hint: Walk through: Situation → Automatic Thought → Emotion. Then ask: Was it all-or-nothing thinking? Catastrophizing? Mind-reading? Overgeneralization? Personalization?

title: Automatic Thoughts: Mental Background Noise - Quiz
